mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑AMG G63 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned G63 engine (M157 or M177) typically costs £6,200–£9,000 supply only depending on generation. A replacement M157.984 engine costs on average £7,908 in good condition. Reconditioned M157 engines start from around £6,200. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500. Labour costs are higher than for conventional engines due to the hot‑vee design and the G‑Class's complex chassis. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑AMG G63 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M157 and M177 are engineering masterpieces, but they are high‑stress, high‑temperature engines with well‑documented weaknesses. The M177 suffers from rear main seal and oil separator failure – 'one of the most frequent and expensive problems'. Oil leaks, turbocharger issues, and cylinder scoring are also concerns. With rigorous maintenance (oil changes every 8,000‑10,000 miles), these engines can be durable – but repairs are expensive. As one specialist noted, 'Issues are possible but not probable. It's likely that the resulting repairs be more expensive than a less expensive engine in a less expensive car'.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑AMG G63?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the M177 (W464, 2019‑present), the most common issues are rear main seal and oil separator failure (causing oil leaks), turbocharger wear (reduced boost, lag), oil leaks from turbo inlet and oil cooler seals, and cylinder scoring (misfire, loss of compression). On the M157 (W463, 2013‑2018), common issues include oil leaks, turbocharger failure, and exhaust manifold leaks.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a G63 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Given the G63's exceptional retained value (typically £80,000–£150,000+), a £6,200–£9,000 reconditioned engine is a modest fraction of the vehicle's worth. Replacement is almost always financially viable. However, if the engine has suffered catastrophic failure (cylinder scoring, snapped timing chain), the total repair cost may be substantial. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my G63's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W463 (2013‑2018) G63 uses the M157.984 – a 5.5‑litre V8 biturbo. The W464 (2019‑present) G63 uses the M177.980 – a 4.0‑litre V8 biturbo. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C. The 2024‑onward models feature a 48V mild‑hybrid system.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a G63 engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – rear main seal/oil separator failure (if caught early), turbocharger issues, oil leaks – are repairable. However, if cylinder scoring has occurred or the engine has suffered major internal failure, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild. A rear main seal replacement on an M177 can cost 'well into the thousands'.

585 PS, 850 Nm — handcrafted M177, 48V mild‑hybrid from 2024, rear main seal and oil separator failures are defining weaknesses

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page must match. Sections 2, 4, and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

rows.bestFor: Insurance jobs; warranty claims; full provenance

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the G63 AMG pricing across generations. A replacement M157.984 engine costs on average £7,908 in good condition, with new or reconditioned units costing up to £7,930. Reconditioned M157 engines start from around £6,200. Reconditioned M177 engines are at the upper end of the range. Labour costs are higher than for conventional engines due to the hot‑vee design and the G‑Class's weight and packaging.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a G63 AMG engine typically takes 16‑22 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist). The hot‑vee design and the G‑Class's heavy, complex chassis add significant labour complexity. A rear main seal replacement on an M177 can cost "well into the thousands".

problems.title: M177 Rear Main Seal & Oil Separator Failure (W464, 2019–present)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, visible oil leak from between the engine and transmission bell housing, increased oil consumption. As these vehicles accumulate mileage, one of the most frequent and expensive problems encountered is oil separator failure, which will often lead to rear main seal leaks.
problems.typicalMileage: 30,000–60,000 miles — can occur on low to mid‑mileage cars.
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£4,000+ [PLACEHOLDER] (rear main seal and/or oil separator replacement — gearbox removal required).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored oil loss can lead to engine damage).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected or oil consumption increases.

problems.recommendation: The M177 engine is prone to oil separator failure, which leads to rear main seal leaks. Both excessive positive pressure and excessive vacuum can damage seals, gaskets, and other engine components. Proper crankcase pressure balance is critical to the long‑term health of the M177 engine. If you notice oil spots under your G63 or a burning oil smell, have the oil separator and rear main seal inspected immediately. This is a labour‑intensive repair that requires gearbox removal — costs can run "well into the thousands".

problems.title: M177 Turbocharger Issues (W464, 2019–present)
problems.symptoms: Turbo lag, reduced boost pressure, loss of power, whistling or grinding noise from the engine bay, engine warning light, excessive exhaust smoke. Issues such as turbo lag, reduced boost pressure, or even premature wear of turbine components are not uncommon.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored turbo failure can send debris into the engine).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you notice power loss or unusual noises.

problems.recommendation: The M177's twin turbochargers are critical in delivering the engine's substantial boost pressure. However, they are sensitive components that operate under intense thermal and mechanical stress. The M177 tends to have high oil and water temperatures even in series production. Regular inspections and timely servicing of the turbo system can help mitigate problems. If you notice turbo lag, reduced boost, or unusual noises, have the turbos inspected immediately.

problems.title: Oil Leaks — Turbo Inlet Seal & Oil Cooler Seals (M157 & M177)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, visible oil leak from the engine bay, rising oil temperature, limp mode.
problems.repairCost: £500–£2,000 [PLACEHOLDER] (turbo inlet seal or oil cooler seal replacement).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected.

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are one of the more frequent complaints with the M177 engine, often stemming from poorly sealed gaskets or oil pan issues. Turbo inlet seal leaks and oil cooler seal leaks are also common. The high operating temperatures and pressures cause increased oil consumption over time. Ignoring oil leaks can lead to accelerated engine wear, turbo failure, sludge buildup, and in worst cases, bearing damage or engine seizure. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, investigate immediately.

problems.title: M157 Exhaust Manifold / Header Leaks (W463, 2013–2018)
problems.symptoms: Ticking or tapping noise from the engine, especially when cold, exhaust smell in the cabin, reduced performance, engine warning light.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection soon if you hear ticking or notice exhaust smell.

problems.recommendation: The M157's exhaust manifolds can develop leaks due to the extreme heat cycles of the hot‑vee design. Look for oil leaks around the passenger side manifold. A leaking manifold can cause ticking noises, exhaust fumes in the cabin, and reduced performance. If you hear ticking or notice an exhaust smell, have the manifold gaskets inspected.

problems.title: M177 Cylinder Scoring (W464, 2019–present)
problems.symptoms: Misfire, loss of compression, rough running, engine warning light, increased oil consumption.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice misfire or rough running, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: Mercedes has a tradition of cylinder scoring issues on engines. The surface coating can fail, causing scuffing and eventual loss of compression and misfire. Hard‑driven engines can have cylinder scoring. Proactively changing oil no greater than every 10,000 miles is essential. If you experience misfire or rough running, have the engine inspected immediately — cylinder scoring can be catastrophic.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes‑AMG G63 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.problem: M177 rear main seal / oil separator failure
rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance — always worth doing on a high‑value vehicle
Worth fixing — common on high‑stress turbo engines
Worth fixing — common on the M157
Depends on damage extent — may require new head or engine replacement
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes‑AMG G63? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles)
* W464 (M177): Oil separator and rear main seal inspected — this is critical
* W464 (M177): No oil leaks from the engine — turbo inlet and oil cooler seals should be dry
* W463 (M157): Exhaust manifolds inspected and no ticking noises
* A well‑maintained example — the G63 is a high‑value, high‑performance vehicle that demands attention
avoidIf: * W464 (M177): Any sign of oil leaks — rear main seal or oil separator issues
* W464 (M177): Any sign of turbo noise or reduced boost — turbocharger warning
* W463 (M157): Any sign of ticking from the engine — exhaust manifold warning
* Any sign of misfire or rough running — cylinder scoring warning
* Poor service history — the M157 and M177 are intolerant of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes‑AMG G63 is the ultimate luxury off‑roader — a handcrafted V8 biturbo monster wrapped in iconic, boxy styling. The M157 (2013‑2018) and M177 (2019‑present) are engineering masterpieces, but they are high‑stress, high‑temperature engines with well‑documented weaknesses — rear main seal and oil separator failure (M177), turbocharger issues, oil leaks, and cylinder scoring. As one specialist noted, "Issues are possible but not probable. It's likely that the resulting repairs be more expensive than a less expensive engine in a less expensive car". Buy on service history above all else — a well‑maintained G63 is an extraordinary and rewarding vehicle; a neglected example can be ruinously expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare G63 AMG engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑AMG G63 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Oil Leaks — Check under the vehicle and around the engine for any signs of leakage (rear main seal, turbo inlet, oil cooler)
2. Cold Start — Any ticking or rattling? (exhaust manifold or timing chain warning)
3. Service History — Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
4. Turbo Health — Any whistling, lag, or smoke? (turbocharger warning)
5. Engine Serial Number — M177: Check for any known issues with specific production batches
6. Running Quality — Any misfire or rough running? (cylinder scoring warning)
7. Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles? (oil separator or seal warning)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes

items.question: Section 9: Trust + Final CTA, with exactly three trust points, a final CTA paragraph, and a CTA button that matches the Assertive mode determined in Part 1 (Priority H).
The Meta tags, Open Graph, and Twitter Card with the correct character counts and patterns.
The full JSON-LD schema stack with all required blocks (BreadcrumbList, ItemList for Era Map and Engine Codes, FAQPage, Organization, Service, Dataset, WebPage, and AggregateRating omitted).
items.answer: I must ensure every number carries a tag, every engine code in the schema matches Part 1's Era Map exactly, and the Authority Model confirmation is included in the Production Note. The brand voice should be "Refinement & reassurance" for Mercedes, but honestly, for an AMG model, the voice in the sample pages has been more performance-oriented. I'll stick to the confident, data-led, but refined tone. The user's identity seems to be a content manager, SEO specialist, or developer working on a large automotive content site. They need this output to be ready to publish, with the schema and meta data correctly formatted. The biggest challenge is making sure the schema's Service price range spans the full history (from lowest used to highest rebuilt) and that the engine item lists are identical to Part 1. I should also check the brand-specific constants: Specialist count 25+, Scrap threshold 70%, Data tag MB-. Alright, I'm ready to generate the complete Part 2 for the Mercedes-AMG G63.
